text: The surgeon mobilizes the colon away from the kidney, revealing the vena cava running left to right below the scissors. Dissection occurs between the vena cava and Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to elevate the tissue, continuing dissection downward to identify the ureter and gonadal vein. The gonadal vein is visible, and the ureter is dissected and clipped. The psoas muscle is noted posterior to the kidney, marking the limit of dissection in a relatively avascular plane. The fourth robotic arm is used to elevate the kidney and ureter during the dissection.

The surgeon dissects towards the renal hilum, exposing the renal vein running from the right lower to the left upper part of the field. The renal artery is identified and dissected from surrounding lymphatic tissue, with visible venous branches behind it. The artery is clipped using WAC clips, with two clips placed on each side for security.

The surgeon places a final clip on the renal artery, ensuring vascular control. The artery is well controlled, with no significant additional veins or arteries visible behind it. The kidney is stretched slightly to provide a clear line for the application of a vascular load Ethicon stapler, preparing for venous control.

The surgeon spares the adrenal tissue, located north or to the right of the stapler, and carries the dissection laterally towards the side wall. The lateral attachments of the kidney are scored for complete removal. The kidney is placed in a bag and extracted through a small incision near the umbilicus.
